And so virtualization, why are we talking about it? It's a very important concept, partially because it addresses the issue of, you know, remember our distributed system is a collection of components that interact with each other, perhaps on different machines. and an issue that we have not really discussed very much is, you know, how do you deploy those components? How do they run on those machines, okay? We may be very lucky, they may be compiled specifically to run on a specific machine, but maybe we want the ability to be more flexible with how those components get located, where they run, okay?

So virtualization is an important tool, can help us solve some of those problems. Okay. You know, you might not be as used to thinking this way. I mean, you know, you compile a program and you run it on your machine, bare metal, it works. But you're writing a large application, a distributed application, and you want it to persist for a while. And that may go through changing hardware and even changing operating systems. So you want to be able to sort of have your program continue to run perhaps as machines come and go even. The other thing is that certain types of software changes fairly slowly. I'm thinking of like older software, legacy software, that is so great, you have the binary for it and that's all you need. It's just been working for a decade. And so it's pinned to a specific machine, a specific operating system, and I want to run it on a newer system. Okay, how do I do that? And of course, software does change, and hopefully we have compilers for that to change, but we want to be able to run things flexibly on different places is the kind of concept here. So we want to be able to port our code very easily and migrate it to different systems, perhaps even dynamically. And one of the things that virtualization gives us, besides this flexibility, is because it's going to gain that flexibility by remapping interfaces effectively, putting a layer between your program and the system, it is a very nice way to add additional types of isolation over what a normal process gives you. So virtualization generally gives you the property of isolation, meaning what happens in your world, in your workspace, might have very little impact on others. Now processes do that to a certain extent, but not as fully virtualized systems. Similarly, if you attack a component, for example, an operating system, hopefully running in a guest virtual machine, well, you can corrupt that virtual machine, that guest, but it doesn't affect your virtual machine. On the other hand, if you were to running bare metal, like my machine here, and you corrupt the native host, then who knows what will happen to my machine. All my applications won't work properly. So having this other layer can also promote some aspects of security, an

important topic that we'll get to later. And I found this paper pretty interesting. I like when you take complicated concepts and reduce them to very simple ideas. It's one of the things I really like. Virtualization at some level seems like a fairly complex topic. But one of the interesting points the paper makes out is it's really about mimicking interfaces. And that's kind of an interesting idea. Meaning that we want to retain the interfaces we have, in some sense, but then we want somehow flexibly to change those interfaces without having the source program edited to change to a new API, for example. so the interesting thing is this goes back way back to the 1970s flower power was in disco music you know it's probably in YouTube you can probably look it up the great era and before the PC revolution people programmed on larger machines like mainframes for example you may have heard of these and I think I said the first day of class that almost any internet financial transaction touches a mainframe computer somewhere in the back end. in between the program and the new system. And so that's this virtualization layer, which is a mimicking layer. But I want to focus specifically on machine virtualization, hardware virtualization. And so there are three sort of broad categories that you have that we can talk about. So the first one is process virtualization, or sometimes called application virtualization, where the unit of virtualization is the process. We've talked about the process a little bit. It gives you some isolation. Another good example, you can go a little further than a process by further virtualizing that process, such as what Java does. So the Java process, the Java virtual machine, provides a separate set of instructions, separate from what the hardware is, this Java bytecodes that are interpreted. And so the interpreter kind of runs inside the process, interpreting your Java code. You can compile it also, but it will interpret that. Okay? And no other process, you know, interacts with this Java process, and the Java process doesn't interact with anybody else, but inside the Java process are all these little things running. Okay? Written in some other language. So that's kind of cool, and, you know, Java was super popular still. Runs in a lot of different places because of that model.

As As long as we can have the Java process run on different types of hardware, Java will run everywhere. Then we have what is more classically considered virtualization. It's going a little bit deeper. And in these other models shown in B and C, we have a piece of software called a virtual machine monitor. This is really a layer that has to be inserted to really give you more power, more portability even. So there are two types of those. There's what's called a native virtual machine monitor, and then there's a hosted virtual machine monitor. And so here's the picture here. So this is sometimes called type 1 or bare metal because it sits right on top of the hardware. So it effectively acts as a full -fledged operating system on its own. But it has some additional features to let it interact with different operating systems that may be contained in guest virtual machines running on top of it. So what this guy does, as you can look below, is it virtualizes this hardware. Okay. So you can have your application and your own OS together running, and your OS can handle most of the system calls, except ones that affect hardware and are privileged. Then what happens is the system arranges for those to be trapped because your OS runs as user code, because this is the operating system. One of the user code will trap to the virtual machine monitor, which will emulate those system calls, figure out how to execute them. They have to be executed with privilege. This is the only thing that has privilege. And depending on how powerful this is, it may handle various operating systems. It may have that kind of power to do that, completely different operating systems. And then it may have to remap the system calls to something different. Okay, and examples of those are Xen, VMware, a few other things. that's hardware virtualization or bare metal. The second type, which is, I think, probably more common, is hosted. And in hosted, what you have is the standard setup. You have your operating system on top of hardware, Linux or Windows, and then you have a virtual machine monitor that runs on top of that. So it needs to do less heavy lifting than a hosted operating system like down here, but

it has to essentially virtualize not only the hardware, but also the operating system. And then, like here, you get to write your own application using your own operating system as a guest. And this is sometimes called system virtualization, because if you look down, you see operating system and hardware kind of below the virtual machine monitor.
